sections.title: Bone Counts (Important!)
points: A normal adult human being has 206 bones.
A newborn baby has more: between 300 to 305 bones.
Why? Babies are born with many cartilages that later join/fuse together as they grow.
Ossification: The process where cartilages turn into hard bones using minerals.

sections.title: Understanding Girdles
points: Pectoral Girdle: The shoulder bones (Scapula and Clavicle) that attach the arms to the spine.
Pelvic Girdle: The hip bones that attach the legs to the spine.

sections.title: What do the bones protect?
points: Skull (Cranium): Protects the Brain, Eyes, and Middle/Inner ears.
Pelvic Girdle (Hip): Protects the bladder and Reproductive organs (e.g., Uterus in women).

sections.title: Living Tissue Facts
points: Bones are NOT dead; they have blood supply and nerves to feel pain.
The Red Bone Marrow acts as a factory for making Red Blood Cells.
Bones act as a 'bank' to store Calcium and Phosphorus.

sections.text: Record Breakers: The Femur (thigh bone) is the longest and strongest bone! The Stapes (in the ear) is the smallest and lightest bone in the body.

cards.mnemonic: Babies are born with cartilage that fuses into bone!

cards.fact: The 80 bones in the center of your body (Skull, Spine, Ribs).
cards.mnemonic: Think of an Axle—it's the middle pole everything turns on!

cards.fact: The 126 bones of the limbs and girdles.
